title: The Illuminated Bestiary — The Manananggal: An Account from the Visayan Record
cultural_origin: Visayan (Western Visayas — the island of Panay, especially Capiz, Iloilo, Antique, and Aklan), Philippines; lowland Christianized Visayan folk tradition with pre-Hispanic Austronesian roots, understood locally as one named branch of the broader aswang complex

shots.narration: Before we begin, understand what this is. Not a story told to frighten children. Testimony. Field reports gathered across the central Philippine islands from people who never met, who reached for the same Visayan word to name the same thing. That word is tanggal. It means to detach. To take a body apart.
shots.prompt: Extreme close-up. A weathered human hand lifts the corner of a heavy, ancient leather cover. A single thick vellum page turns mid-frame, catching low raking candlelight from the side. Deep chiaroscuro, ink texture visible, the darkness beyond the page total.
shots.style_medium: a genuine antique illuminated codex page in the Hispano-Philippine colonial manuscript tradition, egg tempera and weathered gold leaf on aged vellum, heavy ink linework, hand-ruled margins, 17th-century mission-workshop hand

shots.narration: Hold onto that word, because the whole account turns on it. This is not a creature described by what it looks like. It is defined by an act — a separation. Everything else in the record hangs off that single verb.
shots.prompt: The chapter heading fills the frame, lettered in archaic Visayan script with a gilded initial. The camera settles on the page as the ink seems to deepen in the guttering light.
shots.style_medium: a genuine antique illuminated codex page in the Hispano-Philippine colonial manuscript tradition, egg tempera and weathered gold leaf on aged vellum, heavy ink linework, hand-ruled margins, 17th-century mission-workshop hand

shots.narration: Now the geography, because in this case location is the first piece of evidence. The accounts cluster hard in the Western Visayas — the island of Panay. And within Panay, one province is named again and again: Capiz.
shots.prompt: Top-down hand-tinted ink map of Panay Island, coastlines in faded sepia, a prominent compass rose in the corner. Capiz is outlined in heavy dark charcoal ink, the other provinces in lighter wash. Stained parchment texture, warm low-key light.
shots.style_medium: a genuine antique illuminated codex page in the Hispano-Philippine colonial manuscript tradition, egg tempera and weathered gold leaf on aged vellum, heavy ink linework, hand-ruled margins, 17th-century mission-workshop hand

shots.narration: For generations, mentioning Capiz elsewhere in the archipelago drew the same reaction as naming a notorious address. The reputation ran deep enough that people from other provinces were wary of marrying into Capiz families. A belief with that kind of social weight leaves marks on real lives.
shots.prompt: The map slowly recedes to reveal it resting on a wooden table, a small oil lamp beside it, a marginal note inked in an old hand along the edge of Capiz. Warm pool of light, surrounding darkness.
shots.style_medium: a genuine antique illuminated codex page in the Hispano-Philippine colonial manuscript tradition, egg tempera and weathered gold leaf on aged vellum, heavy ink linework, hand-ruled margins, 17th-century mission-workshop hand

shots.narration: Local records make a distinction that outsiders almost always flatten. They do not call this an aswang. In Visayan usage, aswang is an umbrella — a whole category of night-feeders, witches, and shapeshifters. The manananggal is one specific type inside it, singled out by one trait: the self-severing.
shots.prompt: A clinical manuscript diagram. Abstract black ink silhouettes joined by branching lines, mapping a classification tree. One single branch glows with a faint wash of gold leaf; the others fade into smoky ink shadow at the margins.
shots.style_medium: a genuine antique illuminated codex page in the Hispano-Philippine colonial manuscript tradition, egg tempera and weathered gold leaf on aged vellum, heavy ink linework, hand-ruled margins, 17th-century mission-workshop hand

shots.narration: So keep the categories clean. Blur them and you lose the very thing the informants were careful to preserve. They knew the difference between a creature that changes shape and a creature that comes apart.
shots.prompt: The diagram's illuminated branch isolated against near-black, the single gilded line leading to a small featureless silhouette of an upper torso. Restrained, almost heraldic.
shots.style_medium: a genuine antique illuminated codex page in the Hispano-Philippine colonial manuscript tradition, egg tempera and weathered gold leaf on aged vellum, heavy ink linework, hand-ruled margins, 17th-century mission-workshop hand

shots.narration: Begin with the daylight description, which is unremarkable, and that is the point. By day she is an ordinary neighbor. Often a midwife. Sometimes a weaver, or a trader at the market. Always someone known by name.
shots.prompt: Eye-level portrait. An ordinary 17th-century Visayan woman in a plain textile camisa, standing still in the dark doorway of a bamboo hut at dusk. Split lighting: one side of her calm face catches faint evening glow, the other lost in the pitch-black interior behind her.
shots.style_medium: a genuine antique illuminated codex page in the Hispano-Philippine colonial manuscript tradition, egg tempera and weathered gold leaf on aged vellum, heavy ink linework, hand-ruled margins, 17th-century mission-workshop hand

shots.narration: In small barangays where everyone knew everyone else's business, that detail mattered. The threat here is never the stranger from the forest. It is the familiar face two houses over. Suspicion always had an exact door to land on.
shots.prompt: The same woman now seen from behind, a narrow village lane of nipa houses stretching ahead of her in fading light, other doorways dark and watchful along both sides.
shots.style_medium: a genuine antique illuminated codex page in the Hispano-Philippine colonial manuscript tradition, egg tempera and weathered gold leaf on aged vellum, heavy ink linework, hand-ruled margins, 17th-century mission-workshop hand

shots.narration: The separation, the accounts agree, happens only after dark. The upper body detaches at the waist. The lower half is left behind — upright, stationary, standing in the corner of the room where she stepped out of it. The horror of the account is not what she becomes. It is what she leaves.
shots.prompt: Low-angle wide shot, extreme low-key. The dark corner of a nipa hut interior. A fully-clothed lower half, legs wrapped in a traditional skirt, stands perfectly still. No wound, no gore — pure silhouette geometry, an unsettling absence. A single blade of blue moonlight slices through a bamboo wall gap across the floor dust.
shots.style_medium: a genuine antique illuminated codex page in the Hispano-Philippine colonial manuscript tradition, egg tempera and weathered gold leaf on aged vellum, heavy ink linework, hand-ruled margins, 17th-century mission-workshop hand

shots.narration: The upper half takes to the air. The Panay records are specific: large, bat-like, membranous wings unfolding from the shoulder blades. Never feathers. This is the outline people drew when they said they had seen it pass — low and fast, just above the rooflines.
shots.prompt: Wide exterior night landscape. A cluster of sleeping thatched nipa huts under a deep indigo sky, a cold distant crescent moon the only light. Flying low above the rooflines, a massive dark leathery membranous winged silhouette, purely black against the sky.
shots.style_medium: a genuine antique illuminated codex page in the Hispano-Philippine colonial manuscript tradition, egg tempera and weathered gold leaf on aged vellum, heavy ink linework, hand-ruled margins, 17th-century mission-workshop hand

shots.narration: Then a detail that sounds like nonsense until you understand it. The accounts describe a sound that travels with her — a call, tik-tik, or in some districts wak-wak. And the rule is inverted: the call is loud when she is far, and grows faint as she draws near. A warning that fails exactly when you most need it.
shots.prompt: Interior night. A figure lies still under a woven blanket on a floor mat, one hand near the ear. Through the slatted wall, the faint suggestion of something perched close outside — barely more than a denser patch of dark. Absolute stillness, held breath.
shots.style_medium: a genuine antique illuminated codex page in the Hispano-Philippine colonial manuscript tradition, egg tempera and weathered gold leaf on aged vellum, heavy ink linework, hand-ruled margins, 17th-century mission-workshop hand

shots.narration: The target, handled with the restraint the sources themselves used. She preyed above all on the household at its most fragile — women in late pregnancy or newly delivered. The primary accounts settle her on the thatch at midnight and go no further. What matters for the record is the pattern, not the particulars.
shots.prompt: From inside a darkened hut looking up. High-angle on the layered bamboo-and-thatch ceiling, moonlight straining through the cracks. A large clawed silhouette stretches across the ceiling from above — a winged figure crouched on the roof, shown only as shadow, never itself.
shots.style_medium: a genuine antique illuminated codex page in the Hispano-Philippine colonial manuscript tradition, egg tempera and weathered gold leaf on aged vellum, heavy ink linework, hand-ruled margins, 17th-century mission-workshop hand

shots.narration: One anatomical detail holds identical across three hundred years of accounts. A long, thread-like tongue — narrow enough to pass between the gaps of a bamboo-slat floor. That single trait explains the shape of the entire defense that follows.
shots.prompt: Tight low-angle macro. A raised bamboo-slat floor seen from beneath, patches of warm light filtering through the narrow irregular gaps. A single incredibly fine dark thread-like shape probes down through one gap, isolated in the light. Clinical, minimal, unsettling.
shots.style_medium: a genuine antique illuminated codex page in the Hispano-Philippine colonial manuscript tradition, egg tempera and weathered gold leaf on aged vellum, heavy ink linework, hand-ruled margins, 17th-century mission-workshop hand

shots.narration: Because of that tongue, the local defense never focused on doors and locks. It focused on the seams. The gaps between floor slats, the joins in a woven wall, the space beneath the eaves. In these houses, the threat came through the openings a building could not close.
shots.prompt: Interior detail study. Hands press crushed material and ash into the narrow gaps of a bamboo wall by lamplight, sealing the seams. Warm focused light on the hands and the wall, everything beyond it dark.
shots.style_medium: a genuine antique illuminated codex page in the Hispano-Philippine colonial manuscript tradition, egg tempera and weathered gold leaf on aged vellum, heavy ink linework, hand-ruled margins, 17th-century mission-workshop hand

shots.narration: This is where the tradition varies by municipality, and the differences are real, not decorative. In some Panay communities she takes only the unborn. In others she appears at sickbeds and near the dying. Elsewhere the emphasis shifts to blood, or to the breath of a sleeper. Same creature. Different appetite. Each village fearing the loss it could least afford.
shots.prompt: A triptych composite panel, three vertical bands divided by ruled gold lines. Band one: an empty rumpled sleeping mat on a bamboo floor. Band two: a simple sickbed of woven mats. Band three: a tightly shuttered window with faint light leaking. All in rough dark ink washes with minimal gold accents.
shots.style_medium: a genuine antique illuminated codex page in the Hispano-Philippine colonial manuscript tradition, egg tempera and weathered gold leaf on aged vellum, heavy ink linework, hand-ruled margins, 17th-century mission-workshop hand

shots.narration: Now the central countermeasure, and it is the most revealing thing in the whole record — because how a culture fights a monster tells you exactly how it imagined the monster working. To destroy her, you must find what she left behind. Locate the standing lower half while the upper body is away hunting.
shots.prompt: Low-angle medium shot, minimalist. Dark corner of a nipa hut. The stationary fully-clothed lower half stands silently, pushed far to the frame's edge in deep shadow — no wound, no anatomical detail, pure silhouette. In the foreground, lit by a small oil lamp, a plain wooden bowl of coarse white salt, grey ash, and whole garlic cloves.
shots.style_medium: a genuine antique illuminated codex page in the Hispano-Philippine colonial manuscript tradition, egg tempera and weathered gold leaf on aged vellum, heavy ink linework, hand-ruled margins, 17th-century mission-workshop hand

shots.narration: Pack the open seam of the waist with salt, grey ash, and garlic. The goal is to ruin the join, so the two halves can never fuse again. And there is a clock on all of it. If she cannot rejoin before first light, the sun destroys the upper half. The whole defense is a race against dawn.
shots.prompt: Close study of the wooden bowl and scattered salt and ash on the floor, a hand mid-motion setting it down. Warm intimate light, the standing lower half only a dark suggestion at the edge of frame.
shots.style_medium: a genuine antique illuminated codex page in the Hispano-Philippine colonial manuscript tradition, egg tempera and weathered gold leaf on aged vellum, heavy ink linework, hand-ruled margins, 17th-century mission-workshop hand

shots.narration: So she was never unkillable. She was structurally dependent on a countdown, and on a body she had to leave defenseless behind her. Reframe her that way and the villagers searching the huts at three in the morning stop being victims. They become hunters working against sunrise.
shots.prompt: Wide exterior pre-dawn. An empty dirt path winds through thatched houses and tall palms. The sky is a cold pale grey, just beginning to lighten at the horizon. A single dim oil lamp carried by an unseen hand throws a small pool of light. Heavy silhouetted atmosphere.
shots.style_medium: a genuine antique illuminated codex page in the Hispano-Philippine colonial manuscript tradition, egg tempera and weathered gold leaf on aged vellum, heavy ink linework, hand-ruled margins, 17th-century mission-workshop hand

shots.narration: There were lighter defenses too, kept ready at every threshold. Braided garlic. Dishes of native coconut vinegar. Lines of salt along the windowsills. Coastal villages hung the dried, barbed tail of a stingray by the door. These were not sacred relics. They were ordinary kitchen supplies, repurposed as blockades.
shots.prompt: Tight low-key still-life. A rough weathered wooden windowsill. A small unglazed earthen dish holds a cloudy liquid catching faint light, beside a neat heap of salt. On the wall behind hangs a large cured dried stingray tail with visible barbs, casting a spike-like shadow. The window beyond is pure black.
shots.style_medium: a genuine antique illuminated codex page in the Hispano-Philippine colonial manuscript tradition, egg tempera and weathered gold leaf on aged vellum, heavy ink linework, hand-ruled margins, 17th-century mission-workshop hand

shots.narration: Detection had its own methods. The village healer — the albularyo — was said to prepare a special coconut oil that grew agitated and bubbled when one of these creatures drew near, pointing toward the source like a needle. Whether it worked is beside the point. It tells you people believed the thing could be found, named, and cornered.
shots.prompt: Close study of a small clay vessel of pale coconut oil on a mat, lit from one side by lamplight, the surface faintly disturbed. A healer's weathered hands hover just above it. Deep surrounding shadow, focused intimate glow.
shots.style_medium: a genuine antique illuminated codex page in the Hispano-Philippine colonial manuscript tradition, egg tempera and weathered gold leaf on aged vellum, heavy ink linework, hand-ruled margins, 17th-century mission-workshop hand

shots.narration: And the accounts describe how the condition passed on. It was not thought to strike at random. In the belief, it was inherited within a family, or transferred deliberately near the end of life — the dying one unable to let go until the affliction was handed to an heir, often through the breath or a whispered object. A curse with a lineage.
shots.prompt: Somber interior. Two seated figures rendered as dark silhouettes facing each other in lamplight, one older and bent, one younger, an unspoken transfer between them. Their features lost to shadow, only posture legible. Heavy stillness.
shots.style_medium: a genuine antique illuminated codex page in the Hispano-Philippine colonial manuscript tradition, egg tempera and weathered gold leaf on aged vellum, heavy ink linework, hand-ruled margins, 17th-century mission-workshop hand

shots.narration: Now step back, because here the folklore turns into something colder. In an age of high infant and maternal mortality, this tradition gave a name and a face to losses that had no other explanation. And the tragedy in the archive is that the face was almost always a real person — an eccentric widow, an outsider, the midwife who happened to be present when a birth went wrong.
shots.prompt: Somber wide-angle interior, extreme low-key. A simple bamboo birthing hut, empty and quiet, an empty hanging woven cradle the central focal point. Against a dark shuttered window in the distance, the faint ambiguous shadow of a woman's silhouette, still and unreadable. Mournful, dark ink tones.
shots.style_medium: a genuine antique illuminated codex page in the Hispano-Philippine colonial manuscript tradition, egg tempera and weathered gold leaf on aged vellum, heavy ink linework, hand-ruled margins, 17th-century mission-workshop hand

shots.narration: The colonial-era clergy recorded these beliefs too, filing them under superstition to be stamped out — and in the recording, preserved them. What they meant to erase, they archived. Much of what we can attest today survives precisely because someone once wrote it down to condemn it.
shots.prompt: A manuscript page within the manuscript: a mission chronicle, dense old script, a marginal drawing of a winged shape scored through with a disapproving ink line. Candlelight across the vellum, a quill resting in the gutter.
shots.style_medium: a genuine antique illuminated codex page in the Hispano-Philippine colonial manuscript tradition, egg tempera and weathered gold leaf on aged vellum, heavy ink linework, hand-ruled margins, 17th-century mission-workshop hand

shots.narration: And the case file never closed. The image moved into mid-century print, into radio dramas, into film, and into contemporary reports across Panay. It remains a framework people reach for when a sudden death or a disturbance in the night resists an ordinary explanation. The account is still being added to.
shots.prompt: Wide night landscape composite. A mix of traditional nipa huts and newer corrugated tin-roofed homes under a bruised indigo sky, a low crescent moon on the horizon. Kept entirely as a black cutout silhouette, the familiar bat-winged upper torso passes silently over the mixed-era rooftops.
shots.style_medium: a genuine antique illuminated codex page in the Hispano-Philippine colonial manuscript tradition, egg tempera and weathered gold leaf on aged vellum, heavy ink linework, hand-ruled margins, 17th-century mission-workshop hand

shots.narration: So set the evidence side by side. A creature named for separation. A body left standing while the rest of it hunts. A defense built entirely on salt, ash, and the coming dawn. The internal logic of the Visayan record points to one thing. The fear was never really the wings, or the flight. It was the vulnerability of the house itself — and the terrible closeness of everyone inside a small community's walls.
shots.prompt: Cinematic pull-back. The illuminated manuscript page fills the frame. The weathered hand re-enters, resting on the dark text for a beat, then slowly and deliberately closes the heavy leather cover over the page. The raking candlelight fades to black as the cover falls.
shots.style_medium: a genuine antique illuminated codex page in the Hispano-Philippine colonial manuscript tradition, egg tempera and weathered gold leaf on aged vellum, heavy ink linework, hand-ruled margins, 17th-century mission-workshop hand
